preference-based bandit problem is framed as a system presenting queries as pairs of options, whereas binary choices are mentioned as popular because they are easy to implement and impose low cognitive load on users.
researchers have considered binary choices to be popular and easy to implement but lacking in revealing preference strength.

researchers have incorporated both additional explicit human feedback, such as ratings, labels, and slider bars, and implicit human feedback, specifically response times, to enhance the understanding of preference strength in decision-making processes.
the context mentions how an online retailer presents users with binary queries regarding a recommended product, highlighting user interaction with online platforms.
users are repeatedly presented with queries about whether to purchase or skip recommended products, emphasizing the decision-making process in online shopping scenarios.
Response time can help overcome the limitation of accurately inferring user preferences when there is a lack of variation in choices, as it shows an inverse relationship with preference strength.

Psychological research indicates an inverse relationship between response time and preference strength, where longer response times may indicate weaker preferences and quick responses may indicate strong preferences.
Response time can uncover subtle differences in preference strength, even when choices appear similar, helping to accelerate preference learning.
Psychological research has extensively studied the relationship between human choices and response times, using complex models like Drift-Diffusion Models and Race Models.

Complex models like Drift-Diffusion Models and Race Models align with both behavioral and neurobiological evidence, and they rely on computationally intensive methods to estimate underlying human utility functions from human choices and response times.
The computationally intensive methods required by complex models like Drift-Diffusion Models and Race Models make them impractical for real-time interactive systems.
Response times are mentioned as impractical for real-time interactive systems
Response times are leveraged to transform binary choices into richer continuous signals
Response times complement choices in utility estimation and provide additional information about preference strength
Faster estimators typically estimate utility functions for a single pair of options without aggregating data across multiple pairs, limiting their ability to leverage linear utility functions
A computationally efficient method is proposed to estimate linear human utility functions from both choices and response times, grounded in the difference-based EZ diffusion model
The proposed method for utility estimation is compared to traditional logistic regression methods that rely solely on choices
Response times complement choices, particularly for queries with strong preferences.

Our linear-regression-based estimator integrates seamlessly into algorithms for preference-based bandits with linear human utility functions, enabling interactive learning systems to leverage response times for faster learning.
Our utility estimator incorporates both choices and response times.
We specifically integrated our estimator into the Generalized Successive Elimination algorithm for fixed-budget best-arm identification.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to integrate response times into bandits (and RL).
Our linear-regression-based estimator integrates seamlessly into algorithms for preference-based bandits with linear human utility functions.

Section 3.1 introduces a new estimator, the “choice-decision-time estimator,” which uses both choices and decision times, in contrast to the commonly used “choice-only estimator” that only uses choices.
Sections 3.2 and 3.3 theoretically compares these estimators, analyzing both asymptotic and non-asymptotic performance and highlighting the advantages of incorporating decision times.

Bandit learners strategically sample queries, update estimates of θ∗, and eliminate lower-utility arms in fixed-budget best-arm identification.
The choice-decision-time estimator allows learners to leverage decision times to gain more information from queries with strong preferences, improving bandit learning performance.

node_1: reliance on reliable response time data
edge: Reliance on reliable response time data may be challenging in crowdsourcing settings where participants' focus can vary.
Future work could integrate eye-tracking data into the DDM framework to monitor attention and filter unreliable responses.
